annotations.label: Analytic geometry

anchor.snippet: is the study of geometry using a coordinate system

annotations.note: Analytic geometry as a discipline is meta-mathematical and has no single formalizing declaration.

annotations.label: Coordinate system on the plane

anchor.snippet: the plane is given a coordinate system, by which every point has a pair of real number coordinates

annotations.note: The plane with real coordinates is modeled by `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)`, but a general 'coordinate system' concept is not a dedicated decl.

annotations.label: Cartesian coordinates

anchor.section: Cartesian coordinates (in a plane or space)
anchor.snippet: where each point has an x -coordinate representing its horizontal position

annotations.note: Cartesian coordinates are captured by `EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)` with componentwise projections, but not named as 'Cartesian coordinates'.

annotations.label: Polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: every point of the plane is represented by its distance r from the origin and its angle

annotations.note: `polarCoord` is the partial homeomorphism on ℝ² mapping (r cos θ, r sin θ) ↦ (r, θ).

anchors.snippet: every point of the plane is represented by its distance r from the origin and its angle

annotations.label: Cylindrical coordinates

anchor.snippet: every point of space is represented by its height z , its radius r from the z -axis

annotations.note: A grep for cylindrical coordinates found no declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spherical coordinates

anchor.snippet: every point in space is represented by its distance

annotations.note: Mathlib has no spherical-coordinate chart; 'spherical' matches only refer to geometric spheres.

annotations.label: Locus of an equation

anchor.snippet: any equation involving the coordinates specifies a subset of the plane, namely the solution set for the equation, or locus

annotations.note: The locus/solution set of an equation is expressed via generic set-builder notation `{x | p x}` or `f ⁻¹' {0}`, with no dedicated 'locus' concept.

annotations.label: Line y = x

anchor.snippet: corresponds to the set of all the points on the plane whose

annotations.note: `Set.diagonal α = {p | p.1 = p.2}` is exactly the line y = x for α = ℝ, generalized to any type.

annotations.label: Linear/quadratic equations specify lines/conics

anchor.snippet: linear equations involving x and y specify lines, quadratic equations specify conic sections

annotations.note: Conic sections are not formalized in Mathlib, so this classification proposition has no declaration.

annotations.label: Trivial and single-point loci

anchor.snippet: the trivial equation x = x specifies the entire plane

annotations.note: These are illustrative examples (`Set.univ`, singletons) rather than stated theorems with a formalizing decl.

annotations.label: Equation of a circle

anchor.snippet: is the equation for any circle centered at the origin

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.sphere_zero_eq` proves `sphere 0 r = {x | ∑ x i ^ 2 = r ^ 2}`, the circle equation for n = 2.

annotations.label: Slope-intercept form of a line

anchor.snippet: the equation for non-vertical lines is often given in the slope-intercept form

annotations.note: A non-vertical line y = mx + b is an affine map ℝ → ℝ, but the slope-intercept form itself is not a named decl.

anchors.snippet: the equation for non-vertical lines is often given in the slope-intercept form

annotations.label: Point-normal form of a plane

anchor.snippet: which is the point-normal form of the equation of a plane

annotations.note: Orthogonality `⟪n, x - p⟫ = 0` infrastructure exists, but the point-normal plane equation is not a dedicated declaration.

annotations.label: Linear equation graphs a plane

anchor.snippet: if a , b , c and d are constants and a , b , and c are not all zero, then the graph of the equation

annotations.note: The solution set of a nonzero affine equation is an `AffineSubspace` (hyperplane), but the specific 3D-plane statement is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: if a , b , c and d are constants and a , b , and c are not all zero, then the graph of the equation

annotations.label: General form of a plane

anchor.snippet: is called the general form of the equation of the plane

annotations.note: The 'general form' is terminology for the plane equation and has no formalizing declaration.

anchors.snippet: is called the general form of the equation of the plane

annotations.label: Parametric equations of a line in 3D

anchor.snippet: lines can not be described by a single linear equation, so they are frequently described by parametric equations

annotations.note: `AffineMap.lineMap p₀ p₁ c = c • (p₁ -ᵥ p₀) +ᵥ p₀` is the parametric line, generalized to any affine space.

anchors.snippet: lines can not be described by a single linear equation, so they are frequently described by parametric equations

annotations.label: Quadratic equations graph conic sections

anchor.snippet: the graph of a quadratic equation in two variables is always a conic section

annotations.note: Conic sections are not formalized in Mathlib, so this theorem has no declaration.

anchors.snippet: the graph of a quadratic equation in two variables is always a conic section

annotations.label: Classification of conics by discriminant

anchor.snippet: The conic sections described by this equation can be classified using the discriminant

annotations.note: The discriminant classification of conics is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quadric surface

anchor.snippet: is a 2 -dimensional surface in 3-dimensional space defined as the locus of zeros of a quadratic polynomial

annotations.note: `QuadraticForm`/`QuadraticMap` formalizes the quadratic polynomial, but its zero locus as a geometric quadric surface is not.

annotations.label: Examples of quadric surfaces

anchor.snippet: Quadric surfaces include ellipsoids

annotations.note: Ellipsoids, paraboloids and other named quadric surfaces are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distance between two points

anchor.snippet: the distance between two points ( x 1 , y 1 ) and ( x 2 , y 2 ) is defined by the formula

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.dist_eq` gives `dist x y = √(∑ dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2)`, the planar distance formula for n = 2.

anchors.snippet: the distance between two points ( x 1 , y 1 ) and ( x 2 , y 2 ) is defined by the formula

annotations.label: Angle of a line with the horizontal

anchor.snippet: the angle that a line makes with the horizontal can be defined by the formula

annotations.note: `Real.arctan` provides the inverse tangent used in θ = arctan(slope), but the angle-of-a-line definition itself is not a decl.

anchors.snippet: the angle that a line makes with the horizontal can be defined by the formula

annotations.label: Distance in three dimensions

anchor.snippet: distance is given by the generalization of the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.note: `EuclideanSpace.dist_eq` is the n-dimensional Euclidean distance formula, specializing to n = 3.

anchors.snippet: distance is given by the generalization of the Pythagorean theorem

annotations.label: Dot product

anchor.snippet: The dot product of two Euclidean vectors A and B is defined by

annotations.note: `Matrix.dotProduct v w = ∑ i, v i * w i` is exactly the dot product, generalized to any ring and index type.

anchors.snippet: The dot product of two Euclidean vectors A and B is defined by

annotations.label: Transformation of a parent function

anchor.snippet: Transformations are applied to a parent function to turn it into a new function with similar characteristics

annotations.note: The pedagogical notion of transforming a 'parent function' has no formalizing declaration.

annotations.label: Standard graph transformations

anchor.snippet: is changed by standard transformations as follows

annotations.note: Standard shifts/scalings/reflections of graphs are not collected as a formalized transformation table.

annotations.label: Reciprocal parent function

anchor.snippet: has a horizontal and a vertical asymptote, and occupies the first and third quadrant

annotations.note: The reciprocal function y = 1/x and its asymptote/quadrant description are not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Unit circle relation

anchor.snippet: is the relation that describes the unit circle

annotations.note: x² + y² = 1 is the r = 1, n = 2 case of `EuclideanSpace.sphere_zero_eq`.

anchors.snippet: is the relation that describes the unit circle

annotations.label: Intersection of geometric objects

anchor.snippet: the intersection is the collection of all points

annotations.note: Intersection of geometric objects is modeled by generic set intersection `∩`, with no dedicated geometric decl.

annotations.label: Intersection of two circles

anchor.snippet: might be the circle with radius 1 and center

annotations.note: Solving for the common points of two specific circles is an example with no formalizing declaration.

annotations.label: Intercepts with coordinate axes

anchor.snippet: the intersection of a geometric object with the

annotations.note: The notion of axis intercepts is not formalized as a dedicated concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: y-intercept of a line

anchor.snippet: specifies the point where the line crosses the

annotations.note: The y-intercept of a line has no dedicated formalizing declaration.

annotations.label: Geometric axis

anchor.snippet: Axis in geometry is the perpendicular line to any line, object or a surface

annotations.note: A geometric 'axis' (perpendicular/symmetry line) is not formalized as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Normal

anchor.snippet: a normal is an object such as a line or vector that is perpendicular to a given object

annotations.note: Perpendicularity via `Submodule.orthogonal` and `⟪x, y⟫ = 0` exists, but 'normal vector/line' is not a named geometric decl.

annotations.label: Surface normal

anchor.snippet: a surface normal , or simply normal , to a surface at a point P is a vector that is perpendicular to the tangent plane

annotations.note: A surface normal requires a tangent plane to an embedded surface, which is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tangent as linear approximation

anchor.section: Spherical and nonlinear planes and their tangents
anchor.snippet: Tangent is the linear approximation of a spherical or other curved or twisted line of a function

annotations.note: The Fréchet derivative `fderiv` is precisely the best linear approximation, though 'tangent' is not itself a decl.

annotations.label: Tangent line

anchor.snippet: the tangent line (or simply tangent ) to a plane curve at a given point is the straight line that

annotations.note: `tangentConeAt` provides tangent-direction infrastructure, but the tangent line as a geometric object is not formalized.

annotations.label: Tangent plane

anchor.snippet: the tangent plane to a surface at a given point is the plane that

annotations.note: `TangentSpace` formalizes the tangent space to a manifold, the abstract analogue of a tangent plane to a surface.
